question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust implemented to support staff affected by the closure of the Endoscopy Reprocessing and Decontamination area in the Downe Hospital in transitioning to new roles or facilities.
answer_text: Although decontamination and cleaning services at the Downe Hospital will transfer, it should be noted that there are no planned changes to remove Bowel Screening Services or indeed any other Endoscopy Services provided at the Downe.  Patients from across the South Eastern Trust area will continue to avail of these services at Downe Hospital.The Trust understands that Downe Hospital staff have supported the decontamination processes for the endoscopy service for a number of years now and have provided an excellent service. The specialist cleaning of the scopes used for endoscopy procedures will, however, need to transfer to the Ulster Hospital site to meet new hygiene standards that cannot be met at the Downe without significant additional investment.There are three staff in the Downe Hospital who undertake the role of cleaning and preparing the scopes as part of their wider duties. The staff have been involved in discussions about the service transfer and they will continue to be involved in conversations about the change.  Most importantly, they have the assurance of maintaining their current jobs as senior Healthcare Assistant/Nursing Auxiliary in the Downe and at their current band.  The changes to decontamination do not involve removing any patient service provision out of the Downe Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) who made the decision to close the Endoscopy Reprocessing and Decontamination area at the Downe Hospital; and (ii) what factors were considered in making this decision.
answer_text: (i) The South Eastern Trust has determined that the specialist cleaning of the scopes used for endoscopy procedures at the Downe will need to transfer to the Ulster Hospital site.  (ii) This service transfer is necessary to meet new hygiene standards that cannot be met at the Downe without significant additional investment.The South Eastern Trust currently has three reprocessing units for endoscopy equipment Trust-wide.  The units in the Ulster Hospital and Lagan Valley Hospital are accredited to ISO 13485:2016, Medicines Health Regulatory Agency.  These two units are managed by the Trust’s Central Sterile Services Department (CSSD) and both meet the standards/guidelines set down by the Department of Health.  Trust CSSD managers are chartered members of the Institute of Decontamination Sciences and meet the standards set down by Health Technical Memorandum 01-06.The Downe unit is not accredited to the ISO 13485 standard which is required by all guidance as best practice. Downe Hospital would have to meet all aspects of all the guidance to continue decontamination services. Upgrading the decontamination services at Downe to the new standards would require significant additional funding to replace end of life endoscope washer disinfectors (EWDs) and air quality equipment. In addition, the increased managerial/supervisory support etc that is required for the new standards means that CSSD is unable to manage a third endoscopy reprocessing unit in Downe Hospital hence the need to relocate this service to the Ulster Hospital.Although decontamination and cleaning services at the Downe Hospital will transfer, it should be noted that there are no planned changes to remove Bowel Screening Services or indeed any other Endoscopy Services provided at the Downe.  Patients will continue to avail of these services at Downe Hospital

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking in relation to (i) the import and sale of non-native seed species; and (ii) the All-Island Pollinator Plan.
answer_text: (i)  Matters relating to the import and sale of non-native seed species are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State as provided for in regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.(ii) My Department is directly responsible for delivery of three actions listed in the All Ireland Pollinator Plan (AIPP) and also provides funding for organisations such as Buglife and Butterfly Conservation, which undertake actions to support the AIPP. My officials support and co-ordinate guidance, publications and events to promote AIPP actions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what procedures they have in place to table applicability motions for new EU acts in line with the timescales set out in paragraph 19 of Schedule 6B of the Northern Ireland Act.

To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has plans to develop a new flood relief fund for non-occupant landlords whose premises were damaged by flooding, who have been unable to obtain insurance cover through no fault of their own and, therefore, not eligible to claim from all existing funding schemes.
answer_text: My Department has worked at pace to create a package of flood support measures aiming to assist as many impacted businesses as possible, and I am pleased that the local Councils have begun to accept applications to the Flooding Hardship Scheme in addition to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme. As the local Councils are responsible for the administration of the Schemes, any questions around eligibility are for the local Councils to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his Department’s plans to make changes to the driving test for lorry drivers.
answer_text: I currently have no plans to make changes to the driving test for lorry drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to ensure continuity of GP services at Rathfriland Health Centre, given that one of the three GP services located there will be ending their contract as of 31 August 2024.
answer_text: On 28 February 2024, Drs Wade and Toner Practice notified the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH) that they were withdrawing from their contract to deliver General Medical Services.  Following completion of the notice period, their contract will end on 31 August 2024.   The SPPG has commenced the process to secure alternative arrangements for these services.  The preferred option is to secure a GP contractor or grouping of GPs to take over the practice through a formal recruitment process.  This is in the early stages and the outcome is unknown at present.  Drs Wade and Toner Practice will continue to deliver GP services and patients do not need to take any action.  They should continue to contact their practice as normal.   SPPG has commenced the process of informing patients and they should be receiving an information letter week commencing 11 March 2024.  To ensure stability for all GP practices in the area, the SPPG has temporarily suspended the ability of the patients of Drs Wade and Toner Practice to change GP practices.  This is only a temporary move until a new contractor is appointed and services are stabilised.  Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts, with the support of Trusts and GP Federations in taking on contracts, the Department has been able to ensure that no practice has closed and that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the disparities in waiting times for practical driving test appointments between large driving school companies and smaller independent driving instructors.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not provide Category B (private car) practical driving test appointments to driving schools.  Driving test appointments are open to all driving test candidates on a fair and equitable basis and can be booked via DVA’s online booking system at Book your practical driving test online | nidirect.The DVA releases Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month.  Additional test slots will also be released as resources continue to become available.For Category C (lorry) and Category D (bus) driving tests, the DVA works closely with driving schools to schedule their candidates for test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the charges and penalties associated with cancelling a practical driving test appointment; and (ii) the procedures in place to manage cancellations and the rebooking of appointments effectively.
answer_text: Practical driving test appointments can be changed or cancelled, without attracting a charge or penalty by providing three clear working days’ notice (working days do not include Saturdays, Sundays or Public Holidays).  Customers who do not provide sufficient notice or fail to attend their appointment will lose their practical driving test fee.Information on how to view, change or cancel a practical driving test appointment is available on NI Direct at Change, cancel or view your practical driving test online | nidirect. Customers can book a practical driving test appointment online at Book your practical driving test online | nidirect or by telephone by calling 0300 200 7862, lines are open 8am - 5pm, Monday to Friday.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what measures are in place to prevent the block booking of practical driving tests by large companies; and (ii) how his Department ensures fairness of accessibility for all driving instructors and learners.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not provide Category B (private car) practical driving test appointments to driving schools.  Driving test appointments are open to all driving test candidates on a fair and equitable basis and can be booked via DVA’s online booking system at Book your practical driving test online | nidirect.The DVA releases Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month.  Additional test slots will also be released as resources continue to become available.For Category C (lorry) and Category D (bus) driving tests, the DVA works closely with driving schools to schedule their candidates for test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to reduce waiting times for a practical driving test appointment.
answer_text: Since the resumption of driving test services following the pandemic the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased their driving test capacity by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional driving examiners, use of overtime to rota off-shift dual role examiners to provide further capacity and cover for leave, they also offer driving test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test. In 2022-2023, the DVA conducted 68,540 driving tests, which is the highest number of driving tests conducted since 2009. Almost 82% (56,132) of all driving tests in 2022-2023 were Category B (private car) tests. Disappointingly, 2,812 people failed to attend their test, which is the highest number on record. The DVA is in the process of recruiting new full time driving examiners who on successful completion of their training will be allocated to test centres from late March 2024 onwards. The DVA releases Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month. Additional test slots will also be released as resources continue to become available.Due to the demand for services the advice to customers, is to book and pay for the earliest date available and check the booking system regularly for an earlier date as additional appointment slots are added when resources become available and when cancellations are made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the waiting times for driving test appointments, broken down by test centre.
answer_text: The average waiting times for driving test appointments broken down by test centre from 1 April 2023 to 18 March 2024 are:		Test CentreAverage daysAltnagelvin42Armagh45Ballymena53Balmoral49Coleraine45Cookstown73Craigavon34Dill Road, Belfast74Downpatrick83Enniskillen35Larne68Lisburn68Mallusk32Newbuildings*7Newry54Newtownards54Omagh32*Newbuildings only conducts bus and lorry driving tests, which are scheduled through standing appointments with local driving schools.These waiting times represent unvalidated management information taken from DVA’s booking system at 14:00 on Tuesday 19 March 2024.  Waiting times regularly fluctuate based on customer demand and the availability of staff resources to conduct driving tests.The DVA publish headline statistics on the number of vehicle, driver and theory tests conducted on a monthly basis, which are available at Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure who is responsible for road defects that are a result of works undertaken by utility providers.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order), forms the legislative framework for street works by utility providers (statutory undertakers). The Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads (2nd Edition), a code of practice approved by the Department under the Order details the utility reinstatement guarantee period.  The permanent guarantee period begins on registration of the permanent reinstatement and runs for a minimum period of two years, or three years for deep openings.  Any reinstatement defects identified during the warranty period are notified to the utility company who are responsible for the repair or replacement at their expense.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department's role in (i) approving; and (ii) progressing City Deal projects.
answer_text: My Department is the approving authority for Innovation, Digital, Tourism and Further & Higher Education led City and Growth Deal Projects. Officials from the Department, and its arm’s length bodies, are actively involved in supporting Councils and Project Promoters develop projects that will positively contribute to the Economy. I want to see delivery of the City and Growth Deal programme accelerated and have therefore instructed officials to work more closely with project promoters to bring forward projects through to delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will commence inspections of road repairs carried out by utility providers, to ensure that the works are of a sufficient standard.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order) forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers. The Department has powers to carry out investigatory works to check whether or not a utility company has complied with its duty to reinstate the street. These are detailed in the Code of Practice for Inspections which can be found at:www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/code-of-practice-for-inspections-2nd-edition.pdf Where a utility company fails to reinstate to an acceptable standard, the reinstatement defects are notified to the utility company responsible for the repair or replacement. Where necessary, additional inspections are carried out by my officials, at the utility company’s expense, to ensure the defects have been corrected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has plans to develop agreed standards for the quality of road repairs carried out by utility providers.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order) forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers such as utility providers. The Order requires the undertaker, when reinstating a street, to comply with such requirements as may be prescribed in relation to the specification of materials and the standards of workmanship to be observed. These requirements have been prescribed in the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads (2nd Edition), a code of practice approved by the Department under Article 31 of the Order. Details of this can be found at:www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/nirauc-code-of-practice-specification-for-the-reinstatement-of-openings-in-roads-2nd-edition.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has raised concerns about the two year slippage in the Belfast Region City Deal's Mourne Mountain Gateway Project.
answer_text: My Permanent Secretary has had meetings with the Chief Executive of Newry Mourne and Down District Council to discuss progress on the Mourne Mountain Gateway project. The project is still at an early stage of development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to allocate funding for the Your School, Your Club funding scheme in (i) 2023-24; and (ii) 2024-25.
answer_text: A total of £572,000 has been disbursed through Your School Your Club in 2023/24 for schemes at Rathfriland High School; Devenish College, Enniskillen; Banbridge High School; and Edmund Rice, Newtownabbey.My officials have bid for capital allocation for the year 2024/25, but as yet no budget has been confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the two additional hardship schemes, announced as part of the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme, will be open for applications.
answer_text: On 21 February, I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding. I am pleased to confirm that the two hardship schemes have opened for applications on 13 March.  As with the previous Flood Scheme these new schemes will be administered by the local councils.   Applications for a hardship payment can be made via the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the further support package of the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme, to detail the specific criteria under which (i) businesses with flood insurance are still eligible to apply; and (ii) businesses without insurance are eligible.
answer_text: Businesses with flood insurance are eligible to apply for the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme, subject to the following criteria:At the time of the flooding, the business held business insurance (that covers contents/fixtures & fitting) which excluded flood damage through no fault of their own or; At the time of the flooding, the business held business insurance (that covers contents/fixtures & fitting etc.) which included flood damage, but the insurance company has subsequently refused to pay out on some or all of the flood damage. Where insurance claims have not yet been resolved/finalised, applications will be put on hold until the outcome is known.It will be the applicant’s responsibility to demonstrate eligibility for the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme. The following supporting evidence should be provided to demonstrate eligibility:Where available, evidence that the business sought, but was not able to obtain insurance cover for flooding for their premises. In the absence of supporting evidence, an explanation as to why flood insurance was not in place and why this was not the fault of the applicant should be provided.A copy of business insurance (that covers contents/fixtures & fitting). Where applicable, a copy of the Buildings Insurance Policy. Where available, evidence that the business sought, but was not able to obtain insurance cover for flooding of their premises. In the absence of supporting evidence, an explanation as to why flood insurance was not in place and why this was not the fault of the applicant should be provided.The Enhanced Flood Support Scheme is being administered by Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and all applications will be assessed by the Council against these criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the social value criteria used in the recent Invest NI tender for the Provision of Advice & Guidance on Northern Ireland’s Dual Market Access Framework.
answer_text: The inclusion of Social Value Criteria is required for all commissions with the potential to exceed the regulated thresholds for public procurement.  As an Executive endorsed policy, Procurement Policy Note 01/21 (Scoring Social Value), is mandatory for all Departments and has the status of government policy, rather than guidance.Invest NI chose Social Value Indicator 1.3 for this tender.  This seeks to create employment and/or training opportunities in industries with known skills shortages or in high growth sectors.   It includes options such as training and mentoring for disadvantaged groups and providing work experience.This indicator is consistent with Executive policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will include consideration of a Universal Basic Income trial within the anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland.Universal Basic Income has been raised by stakeholders during the Anti-Poverty Strategy co-design process. I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Strategy and final content will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he intends to issue any guidance for public bodies on how to assess social value criteria when evaluating tenders.
answer_text: The Finance Minister has Executive responsibility for the development of procurement policy and legislation and it is the responsibility of each Minister to ensure these policies are implemented in their Departments.Current policy for public bodies on social value is contained within Procurement Policy Note 01/21 - Scoring Social Value. In addition, the Social Value Unit within the Strategic Investment Board are available to help public bodies to design, implement and monitor social value in contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what financial resource was allocated for Protect Life 2 for the financial years (a) 2019-20; (b) 2020-21; (c) 2021-22; (d) 2022-23; and (e) 2023-24; and (ii) what is the projected spend for Protect Life 2 for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The table below provides details of the financial resources allocated for Protect Life 2 for the financial years (a) 2019-20; (b) 2020-21; (c) 2021-22; (d) 2022-23; and (e) 2023-24. Protect Life 2 ResourceYear19/20£9,230,05620/21£9,284,21421/22£8,794,73522/23£10,150,99623/24£10,129,635(ii) As budgets have not yet been agreed, I cannot provide projected spend for Protect Life 2 for the 2024-25 financial year.Suicide prevention is complex and no single Department, or any other publicly funded body, is wholly responsible for addressing this complex issue and a collaborative approach is required.Investment in other activities across government also contribute through integration of suicide prevention services into broader service provision for marginalised groups such as homeless people, care leavers, people with addictions, victims of abuse and LGBT people. In addition, a number of government departments have directly funded suicide prevention initiatives. The total invested in suicide prevention in recent years is, therefore in excess of the overall DoH funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her legislative programme for the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: At the beginning of this mandate, I agreed a draft legislative programme that aimed to:improve the experiences of children, young people and victims drawn into the justice system;address a range of sentencing policy issues;tackle hate crime; andmodernise the justice system.Delivery of that programme would have involved the development and introduction of six or seven individual Assembly Bills over the course of the full 2022 – 2027 mandate.The loss of two years from the mandate means that it is clearly not possible for me to deliver all of the Bills that were previously proposed for inclusion in my draft programme.I have, therefore, reviewed and re-prioritised my legislative priorities and now plan to bring forward three Assembly Bills during the time remaining in this mandate.They include:a mixed-content Justice Bill 2024 (provisionally titled the Modernisation of Justice Bill) – for planned introduction in June 2024;a Sentencing Bill – for planned introduction by Spring 2025; anda Hate Crime Bill – for planned introduction by March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps his Department is taking to review drug tariffs for community pharmacies in border regions, to remain competitive with pharmacies in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Medicines used in Northern Ireland are subject to the UK medicines regulatory requirements as set down by the UK regulator, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA). Community pharmacies in Northern Ireland are reimbursed for supplying medicines in line with the prices set out in the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff. The Northern Ireland Drug Tariff is currently reflective of the English Drug Tariff.As a separate jurisdiction, the Republic of Ireland has its own medicines regulatory authority and its own processes for reimbursement for community pharmacists.Consequently, Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland operate as two separate medicines markets. However, a prescription for a medicine written in the Republic of Ireland can be dispensed in a community pharmacy in Northern Ireland and vice versa. A prescription from the Republic of Ireland which is dispensed by a community pharmacy in Northern Ireland will be treated as a private prescription transaction and, as such, the cost of the medicine will be reflective of the price of the medicine in Northern Ireland. The community pharmacist will also add on any applicable dispensing fees.In the same way, a prescription from Northern Ireland which is dispensed by a community pharmacy in the Republic of Ireland will be treated as a private prescription and the patient will be charged the cost of the medicines plus any applicable dispensing fees. These prescriptions would be filled at no cost to the patient if presented in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will seek a funding contribution from the Shared Island Initiative towards the A1 Junctions Phase 2 scheme.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to develop a funding package for unadopted developments when (i) the developer no longer exists; and (ii) the bond set aside is insufficient to cover the works needed to meet an adoptable standard.
answer_text: Prior to construction, a developer is required to enter into Agreements with the Department and NI Water to provide the roads, footways and sewers to the standards prescribed in The Private Streets (Construction) Regulations (NI) 1994 to allow adoption.  These agreements are secured by a bond.Should a developer default or be dissolved, the bond may be used by DfI and/or NI Water to complete the road works or sewers.  It is an offence to commence building works without making prior provision for street works costs.Where the bond is not sufficient to bring the development up to an adoptable standard and the developer has ceased trading, DfI and NI Water have no funding mechanism or legal responsibility to bridge the shortfall between the bond and the cost of the necessary works.  In these circumstances the development remains unadopted and responsibility for the site reverts to the frontagers / homeowners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will answer AQW 5580/22-27.
answer_text: On 15 April, I provided my response to you regarding AQW 5580/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's plans to allocate funding to allow the completion of School Enhancement Programme works at St. Mary's Primary School, Barr.
answer_text: The School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at St Mary’s Primary School, Barr, is currently paused at pre- construction tender stage.At present, budget constraints and pressures in regard to SEN placements mean capital funding is not available for the project to proceed to construction. I will continue to make the case for increased investment in the education estate, to allow much-needed projects, including the project at St. Mary’s Primary School, Barr, to be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department plans to alleviate issues relating to (i) congestion; and (ii) speeding in Strangford village.
answer_text: Queues in the Strangford village can occur when demand exceeds the capacity of the ferry, which can be higher during holiday period and weekends.  The marshalling system is brought into operation for the Strangford Ferry during these busy periods which successfully alleviates most of the congestion in Strangford village. As such, my Department has no plans to implement any additional measures at this time.I am advised that the current speed limits in Strangford Village are in line with the Department’s policy “Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland” and considered to be appropriate. If there are any concerns regarding poor driver behaviour then please refer this to the police who is the enforcement authority using their non-emergency number 101

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will include ferry travel in the Translink Future Ticketing System.
answer_text: Translink are implementing a new ticketing system across the bus and rail network operated by Metro, Ulsterbus, Goldliner and Northern Ireland Railways. Translink have no plans to include the ferries in the ticketing project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration his Department has given to implementing a clawback model for community pharmacies, similar to what exists in Scotland.
answer_text: My Department consulted on modifications to the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff in March 2022 including changes to the clawback arrangements. Following this consultation, a working group was established in 2023, which included community pharmacy representatives, to recommend changes to the clawback scales. The intention with the revised scales is that there should be no change to the overall funding. A phased implementation of the new scales is being commenced at 15% of overall clawback with the February 2024 payment to community pharmacies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to review and adjust the drug tariff.
answer_text: My Department consulted on modifications to the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff in March 2022 including changes to the clawback arrangements. Following this consultation, a working group was established in 2023, which included community pharmacy representatives, to recommend changes to the clawback scales. The intention with the revised scales is that there should be no change to the overall funding. A phased implementation of the new scales is being commenced at 15% of overall clawback with the February 2024 payment to community pharmacies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will take steps to embed (i) responsible dog ownership; and (ii) the importance of animal welfare in the curriculum.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Curriculum is designed to be flexible and dynamic.  There are a number of Areas of Learning which give teachers the opportunity to teach children and young people about animal welfare issues.  These include the World Around Us at primary level and Local and Global Citizenship at post-primary level.My officials have met with officials from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) to discuss resources in schools related to DAERA messaging including responsible dog ownership and animal welfare.  It was recognised that a number of organisations produce educational resources for schools relating to animal welfare and other DAERA key messages.  Work is ongoing to determine if further resources can be produced to support schools in relation to these topics.  However, it is entirely a matter for individual schools to determine which resources they wish to use to support their delivery of the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plan his Department has to review the clawback arrangements for community pharmacies.
answer_text: My Department consulted on modifications to the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff in March 2022 including changes to the clawback arrangements. Following this consultation, a working group was established in 2023, which included community pharmacy representatives, to recommend changes to the clawback scales. The intention with the revised scales is that there should be no change to the overall funding. A phased implementation of the new scales is being commenced at 15% of overall clawback with the February 2024 payment to community pharmacies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the basic training given to GPs on autism symptoms, emotional coping mechanisms and treatment pathways.
answer_text: The Department is aware that there is inconsistency in the provision of autism training for GPs across Trust areas. The need for improved understanding of the condition by GPs was highlighted throughout the public consultation for the Autism Strategy 2023-2028.In response to this, the Department has allocated funding to take forward a regional project to develop three training videos for GPs which will focus on the following:Providing GPs with improved understanding of autism, the characteristics it can present, and the challenges experienced by autistic people. This video will be delivered by clinical staff.Providing an overview of the referral process for autism assessment and the relevant care pathways, supports and interventions within individual Trusts.Explaining the challenges experienced by autistic people when attending a medical appointment and the simple adjustments which, if provided, can make a difference particularly in relation to communication and the sensory environment.  This video will be delivered by two autistic people.Development of the videos which is currently underway has been co-produced by autistic people.  The project was initiated and led by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust. It should also be noted that some GP practices will also avail of training from relevant organisations.This project is being taken forward in the Delivery Plan 2023-2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail his Department’s plans for the Down High School site, Downpatrick; and (ii) whether he will consider this site for a new single campus building for Knockevin Special School.
answer_text: The Down High School site will be retained by the Education Authority for potential alternative educational use. A review on its potential usage is currently ongoing. This will include possible use for Special Educational Needs accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to upgrade the A1 to improve road safety.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.I remain fully committed to delivering this important road safety project.  The statutory Orders for the scheme were made in summer 2021 and work on the project is continuing through the development of contract documents and Business Case. In recognition of current and future potential funding challenges, my Department is exploring options to deliver the project in smaller phased packages.  We are also considering further interim safety measures that could be introduced in advance of the improvement scheme.Details of the current status and timescales for of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/a1-junctions-phase-2-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will extend automatic rates relief for a further financial year for businesses impacted by flooding in November 2023.
answer_text: Recognising the need to support businesses impacted by flooding in November 2023, my Department is providing 100% rate relief up until 31 March 2024.  The Department for the Economy has announced wider grant provision for those properties still in need of support.The grant provision from Department for Economy is being rolled out to those most severely affected by the flooding so as to provide longer term support or an increased quantum of support. Therefore, there are no plans to extend the rate relief beyond that date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans to has to review the fees paid to nursing homes by private care providers.
answer_text: The Department annually reviews the regional tariff rate for residents placed in residential and nursing care homes. The regional tariff rate is a guide to what the HSC system considers to be fair and affordable, it is not a fixed price for a care placement.  This annual inflationary review process is underway and the sector will be advised as soon as the budget permits.My Department has established a Social Care Collaborative Forum to provide a formal mechanism for officials and representatives of the Social Care Sector (statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors), to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements that will support and sustain social care now and into the future.  As part of its remit, the Forum will review proposals arising out of the consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care including future charging arrangements within the sector. Progress against all aspects of the Forum’s work will be reported on and where specific proposals are being made in respect of charging for care these will be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans his Department has to improve the accountability and governance of boards of governors in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Accountability and governance responsibilities vary across grant aided school sectors and types in Northern Ireland.  As set in legislation, schools are managed by differing managing authority arrangements who are responsible for accountability and governance. The Department is not a managing authority for any granted aided school. The recently published Independent Review of Education included recommendations on the composition and arrangements for Boards of Governors in granted-aided schools in Northern Ireland and my Department is currently considering the recommendations of this report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will meet with Downpatrick and County Down Railway to explore ways of supporting the organsiation to (i) recover after recent flooding; and (ii) continue to grow as Ireland’s only full-sized mainline heritage railway.
answer_text: My Department has accelerated plans to undertake a feasibility study of flood risk for the Downpatrick area to determine the factors that may have contributed to flooding in this area and consider if there are any viable flood risk management, maintenance or capital interventions which can be identified, including in the vicinity of the Downpatrick and County Down Railway. The report will be published when it is complete.While DfI regulate the safety of heritage railways, funding, investment and growth are not within my Department's remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s plans to deliver a park and ride facility for Downpatrick and the surrounding area.
answer_text: A suitable site for a Park & Ride at Downpatrick, has been identified within the Down Business Park along the A7 Belfast Road.  The project has the potential to provide parking for approximately 400 vehicles.  Environmental assessments and detailed designs have been completed.  Once a business case has been approved, the Department will begin the formal submission of a planning application.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps his Department is taking to ensure sports clubs are able to access financial support from the Flood Recovery Fund.
answer_text: On the 21st February I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding.As with the previous Flood Scheme these new schemes will be administered by the local councils. The councils plan to open the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme for applications early next week with the two hardship schemes in the following couple of weeks.   Sport’s clubs operating from premises that were flooded and that were not eligible for the £7,500 scheme will be eligible for a £5,000 hardship payment (as long as other eligibility criteria are met).  Further details on scheme administration, including the eligibility criteria and how to apply, will be provided in the forthcoming week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans his Department has to regulate home heating oil industry in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: If we were to introduce regulations it would require primary legislation to establish the regulatory framework. It is also worth noting that price controls will do little to tackle the underlying causes of oil price fluctuations as the price regulator would have to allow the pass-through of legitimate input costs, most notably the crude oil price. Additionally, the costs of regulation, including administrative and other indirect costs, could add further upward pressure on consumer’s bills.At the present time, the Competition and Markets Authority has jurisdiction to investigate and take action to address anti-competitive practices. In terms of consumer protection against unfair trading practices, my Department’s Trading Standards Services (TSS) has the power to investigate complaints of this nature and can take enforcement action where consumer protection regulations have been breached, including around the sale and delivery of home heating oil.The Executive’s Energy Strategy, “The Path to Net Zero Energy,” sets out a pathway to decarbonising our economy and phasing out fossil fuels. This will end the use of heating oil and serve to protect consumers as we work to decouple all energy sources for heating from the global fossil fuel markets. For these reasons I am not currently planning to regulate the oil industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progression of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.The A24 Ballynahinch Bypass will deliver a 3.1km bypass to the east which will improve traffic conditions in the town centre. Details of the current status of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: A24 Ballynahinch Bypass - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will carry out targeting dredging of the Quoile River, Downpatrick, to improve water quality and reduce flood risk.
answer_text: My Department’s engineers’ have advised that dredging can remove sediment and debris temporarily, but these materials often reaccumulate over time and experience has shown that this is not effective for flooding on the scale recently experienced in Downpatrick.  The Department has accelerated plans to undertake a feasibility study for the Downpatrick area to determine the factors that may have contributed to flooding in this area and consider if there are any viable flood risk management maintenance or capital interventions which can be identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what steps his Department is taking to support the establishment of a Real Living Wage Foundation franchise in Northern Ireland; and (ii) when he expects this support to be rolled out.
answer_text: My Department is exploring the possibility of funding a Real Living Wage Foundation franchise in the region. A business case is being developed on my Department’s ability to fund such a franchise and I expect to receive advice shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 3043/22-27.
answer_text: Please see below response to AQW 3043/22-27 with apologies for the delay.Current Primary Care Talking Therapy Hubs are located within the following areas in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts:TrustLocationBelfastThe Hub team is located within Woodstock Lodge. This team screens and triages referrals received from GPs then refer as appropriate to community and voluntary organisations across the four locality areas within Belfast with whom the Trust hold contracts.NorthernThe Northern HSC Trust does not deliver Primary Care Talking Hubs.   Northern Emotional Well-being Service (NEWS) is a Hub service which is provided by its Community and Voluntary sector partner, LINKS. NEWS operates Trust wide via a locality-based model across the four distinct geographical areas/localities of the Trust – Antrim/Ballymena, East Antrim, Mid-Ulster, Causeway and the Glens.SouthernThe Talking Therapy Hubs are encompassed within the Steps to Wellness Service in the Southern HSC Trust. Steps to Wellness is provided digitally in group work format and is available across all of the Southern Trust area.  South EasternThere are two Hubs which are located in Lisburn and North Down & Ards.WesternThere is one Hub which is situated in Londonderry, Oldbridge House.In terms of the new hubs, preparatory work has been undertaken to review existing models and develop draft proposals for a new hub model. This work is now complete; however additional funding will be required in order to deliver this action under the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy on what date will the second phase of flood recovery funding open for businesses.
answer_text: On 21 February I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding.As with the previous Flood Scheme these new schemes will be administered by the local councils. The councils plan to open the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme for applications early next week with the two hardship schemes in the following couple of weeks. Further details on scheme administration, including the eligibility criteria and how to apply, will be provided in the forthcoming week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps his Department is taking to ensure businesses, previously excluded from financial assistance, will now be eligible for flood relief support.
answer_text: On the 21st February I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding.As with the previous Flood Scheme these new schemes will be administered by the local councils. The councils plan to open the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme for applications early next week with the two hardship schemes in the following couple of weeks. Further details on scheme administration, including the eligibility criteria and how to apply, will be provided in the forthcoming week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will carry out a review of the residential Flooding Support scheme.
answer_text: The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance was introduced by the Executive in 2007. Its purpose is to provide immediate financial support to households who have suffered from flooding. It is not a compensation scheme and does not purport to be one; nor is it intended to replace or displace the assistance that households should receive from their insurers.		At present there are no plans to review the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance for domestic properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to prioritise a review of flood defences in Downpatrick.
answer_text: Following the significant flood which occurred in November 2023, my Department has re-prioritised its work programme and commissioned a civil engineering consultant to undertake a feasibility study for the Downpatrick area.  The study will examine the flooding mechanisms in Downpatrick, identify and assess any potential solutions, and then determine if any solution is viable to alleviate future flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3409/22-27, (i) whether the quoted £7.4 Million is the cost of the MRI machine alone; and (ii) for a breakdown of the cost provided.
answer_text: The MRI scanner and associated costs amount to £7.4m. The following table outlines the breakdown of costs. Project ManagementCost (£m)Works Costs4.07Project Management costs0.22Professional Fees0.59Contingency - Optimism Bias0.67Equipment (scanner)1.85Total Scheme Cost7.40

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of planning applications submitted for significant on-shore wind farms, producing 30 MW and over; and (ii) the length of time it took for planning decision to be made from application submitted to final decision, over the last ten years.
answer_text: The data provided by my Statistics Branch shows that five applications for significant on-shore wind farms that produce 30 MW and over, have been submitted to my Department since 2012 (including those retained from the former DoE).  Three of these applications are yet to be decided and the remaining two applications were decided in 265.20 and 240.40 weeks, respectively.  Over this time period other planning applications for significant on-shore wind farms, producing 30 MW and over, may have been submitted to, and decided by, the relevant council authorities but do not fall within the jurisdiction of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department (i) supports the development of the Northern Mutual Bank for Northern Ireland; and (ii) will meet with representatives from the Northern Mutual campaign.
answer_text: I, and officials in my Department, have followed the development of the Northern Mutual Bank for Northern Ireland with interest. My Department has been represented at meetings of the Northern Mutual Advisory Group and my predecessor, Diane Dodds MLA, held an informative and constructive meeting with the Chair of Northern Mutual Ltd, at which the proposed Mutual Bank was discussed in detail.My Department remains engaged with Northern Mutual Ltd, and in due course, I would certainly welcome the opportunity to build on this through a further meeting with representatives from the Northern Mutual campaign.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3045/22-27, (i) when will the appropriate family engagement arrangements commence; and (ii) will he meet with families as part of these arrangements.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the following questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to put an end to the baiting of (i) badgers; and (ii) foxes, with dogs.
answer_text: Hunting wild animals with dogs is a complex and emotive issue. However, this form of hunting is considered cross cutting in the Executive and it will be for Ministers to agree a way forward in terms of any future legislative proposals.  Badgers are afforded protection in Northern Ireland under the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985, though similar protections are not provided for foxes.   The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) has responsibility to investigate breaches of the Wildlife Order and work closely with DAERA officials who have relevant scientific knowledge to gather information which assists prosecutions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department keeps track of the time it takes to get an appointment to see a Speech and Language Therapist; and (ii) what the average waiting time is to get an appointment in each Health Trust.
answer_text: The Department receives routine information on Speech and Language Therapy waiting lists from the SPPG in the form of active waiting time bands.The number of active waits for a Speech and Language Therapy appointment, as at end of August 2022 (the latest month held) is set out in Table 1.Table 1: Number of active waits for a Speech and Language Therapy appointment, as at 31st August 2022HSC Trust0-3 weeks3-6 weeks6-9 weeks9-13weeks>13 weeksTotalBelfast2402301542451,0771,946Northern1951811361601,1601,832South Eastern2561891302817841,640Southern1941991622481,8312,634Western1361441542437111,388Source: SPPG Monthly AHP Waiting Times. Figures are taken from management systems and are subject to change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to create awareness around the impact that firework displays can have on animal welfare.
answer_text: My Department undertakes a campaign annually, via social media, in the lead up to Halloween to raise awareness of the impact firework displays can have on animal welfare and provide guidance on how to minimise any distress and anxiety that may be caused to them.  My Department will be proceeding with messaging for this year’s campaign shortly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to reduce the waiting time for those seeking Speech and Language Therapy.
answer_text: To date, £69m has been allocated in 2022/23 for additional waiting list activity for the period April to December. The funding is being used to help reduce waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services, including speech and language therapy. The funding is being targeted at those patients with the highest clinical priority and involves creating additional in-house health service capacity over and above normal day to day work, as well as paying independent sector providers to assess and treat patients who are on waiting lists.Bringing our waiting lists to an acceptable level, including looking at innovative ways to manage these across a range of specialties, is a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment and additional staffing.A review of the Allied Health Professions data definitions and elective care pathways is underway to ensure consistency across the region and ultimately improve patient experience. It is expected that this review will be complete by end of March 2023.One expected outcome of this will be to review professional pathways to ensure that there is regional consistency to the delivery of AHP elective services.  This will include reviewing and refreshing existing regionally agreed care pathways, introducing new pathways in specialist areas to enhance the care of patients and maximising the use of virtual consultations. Another expected outcome will be to identify areas of good practice that have impacted positively on capacity and waiting times and share the learning regionally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when a domestic energy efficiency scheme will be announced as noted as objective seven of the Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022.
answer_text: Within the Northern Ireland Energy Strategy, The Path to Net Zero Energy, there is a commitment to deliver an energy efficiency pilot in 2022. In the scoping and development of this initial pilot it has become clear that more urgent action, with much more ambition in terms of scale of intervention, is needed to support people across Northern Ireland. This situation has of course been exacerbated by the current global energy price crisis.With that in mind, my officials are now progressing the development of a multi-year Energy Efficiency Flagship Intervention Programme that will scale up quickly to deliver the volume of retrofits required to both meet our net zero commitments and tackle the cost-of-living crisis. While our initial focus will be on the most vulnerable and the worst performing homes, the programme will then broaden out to take in all homes in Northern Ireland and determine appropriate levels of funding required to ensure this happens.The launch of this flagship will also be a signal for the energy efficiency industry to invest in people and their skills, which in turn will stimulate local economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when will the Utility Regulator provide a response on Northern Ireland Electricity's proposals to socialise grid connection costs for renewable energy installations.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator has commenced a review of connection charging policy and intends to consult as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has plans to launch a support scheme for the purchase and installation of PV solar panel systems on domestic houses.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 2403/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his Department’s plans to introduce new Regulations for offshore wind farms.
answer_text: My Department is participating in UK level discussions relating to the policy proposals outlined in the Offshore Wind Environmental Improvement Package.  These discussions are being led by Defra.  The package is part of the British Energy Security Strategy and the primary legislation needed to implement the proposals is being considered for inclusion in the Energy Security Bill currently going through Westminster. No decision has been made as to whether the proposals relating to offshore wind should apply to Northern Ireland and a Legislative Consent Motion will be required for any of the Energy Bill clauses which relate to devolved matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans his Department has to introduce a renewable energy support scheme, similar to those operational in other UK jurisdictions.
answer_text: As per the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022, my Department intends to consult on renewable electricity support mechanism options this year and begin to deliver a support mechanism for Northern Ireland in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans his Department has to introduce a phased end to greyhound racing in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have no plans to phase out greyhound racing in Northern Ireland. This would not be a decision my Department could take in isolation. Greyhound racing is a hobby and leisure pursuit for owners, supports employment and generates income for our economy. Therefore, while I am aware of the animal welfare concerns that have been raised regarding the pursuit, any decision to ban greyhound racing would cut across the responsibilities of a number of Northern Ireland Departments and could only be taken forward after careful consideration and consultation on all the potential impacts.I would also highlight that those that cause unnecessary suffering to greyhounds can be prosecuted under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 and, if found guilty, can be liable to an unlimited fine and/or up to five years imprisonment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to progress the delivery of an MRI scanner at the Downe Hospital.
answer_text: In 2018, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust submitted a prioritised list of proposals, including the provision of a new MRI scanner for the Downe Hospital, as part of a future capital planning exercise. Unfortunately the project was not affordable within the Department's capital budget at that time, and given the current volatility in the price of material, the Trust has advised the cost of the project has increased to £7.4m. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care including the provision of a new MRI scanner for the Downe Hospital, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. However, my ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Once this project becomes affordable within my Department’s capital budget, the South Eastern Trust will be invited to submit a business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what his Department is doing to ensure users of biomass heating systems are not excluded from any future cost of living support schemes.
answer_text: On 21st September the UK Government (UKG) outlined new plans to help cut energy bills and gave further details on the support available for households in Great Britain and Northern Ireland. 	As part of this announcement, households who are connected to the electricity network but who use alternative fuels other than gas to heat their homes will still receive support through the Energy Price Guarantee for their electricity costs, as well as the Energy Bills Support Scheme. The government has announced that it will provide an additional payment of £100 to NI consumers to compensate for price increases in those alternative fuels. I have also called on UKG to reconsider this level of support for alternative fuel consumers. 		It is vital that these support measures are rolled out as quickly as possible so that local business and domestic consumers are supported as their energy consumption rises into the winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the announcement that substitute teachers pay for September 2022 is being delayed until October 2022, if there will be an emergency payment before October 2022.
answer_text: The majority of temporary teachers, nearly 3,500, received salary payment for days worked in August on the normal payroll date (16 September) however it is regrettable that a number of teachers did not receive timely payment.  My Department does not employ teachers.  The Education Authority (EA) administers the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) on behalf of my Department.  All grant-aided schools can utilise the system to offer bookings to available registered teachers and latterly approve if the teacher accepts and attends.  All bookings approved prior to payroll cut-off dates are transferred in a file from the NISTR to the Department for processing of salary.A short delay in the go-live date of the new NISTR system from 22 to 29 August and performance issues experienced in the first few days of operation resulted in schools being unable to book teachers for August days on the new system in the normal manner.  The EA put extensive additional resources in place to manually input thousands of retrospective bookings on schools’ behalf and to provide assistance to those experiencing difficulties in using the new solution. The Department also extended the August payroll deadline by two days to provide additional time for bookings to be placed, accepted and approved on the system. The main reason teachers did not receive payment for August days related to bookings that not were accepted or signed-off before payroll cut-off.  Additionally, a small number of users experienced technical access issues, bank details had not been provided for some teachers and, in some instances, duplicate bookings resulted in partial payment only. Every potential avenue was explored to provide those affected with an interim payment option, however given the range and complexity of issues, no viable option could be identified.  The impacted teachers will receive payment as part of the next monthly payroll run.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress on the Development Proposal for an integrated college for Mid Down.
answer_text: The statutory two-month objection period for Development Proposal (DP) 667 (Establish a new non-selective, 11-18, co-educational grant maintained integrated post primary school in the Mid Down area with effect from 1 September 2023, or as soon as possible thereafter) and linked proposal 668 (Blackwater Integrated College will discontinue with effect from 31 August 2023, or as soon as possible thereafter) ended on 18 September 2021.  There has been extensive interest in and representation on these proposals and my officials are currently collating all responses received by the Department, along with all other pertinent information and will provide me with a submission to assist me in taking a decision that is in the best interests of all learners.Unfortunately I am unable to offer a specific date for a decision, but I am acutely aware of the need for certainty for the interested parties and I will not delay a decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Fishing and Seafood Development Programme; and (ii) whether there is a date for the commencement of capital works at (a) Ardglass; and (b) Kilkeel harbours.
answer_text: The Department of Finance approved the Strategic Outline Case in October 2021, allowing my Department to incur expenditure on work required to inform the Outline Business Cases (OBC) upon which any later decisions to proceed with infrastructure development will be based.  		To progress this, my Department has been working with Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) of the Department of Finance and the Northern Ireland Fishery Harbour Authority (NIFHA) to develop the tender specifications to procure Integrated Consultant Teams (ICTs) in relation to Ardglass and Kilkeel.  This will be a two stage process and the Pre-Qualification Questionnaire was published on eTendersNI on 17 September 2022. The successful ICTs should be appointed in April 2023. 		The ICTs will undertake environmental and technical studies that are needed to verify the technical viability of the proposed works and develop budget cost estimates.  This will inform the development of the Outline Business Case, which will need approval from the Department of Finance before the decision to commence construction can be taken.		Subject to business case approval, budget allocation, planning permission and a marine licence being granted, construction could potentially commence in spring 2026.  These are indicative timeframes and the ICTs will develop actual work plans for both Kilkeel and Ardglass.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will meet with representatives of family members who are included in the RQIA's Review of the Records of Deceased Patients.
answer_text: The Department has received the final reports for Phase Two from the RQIA and is considering the findings.  The Department and the RQIA are currently making arrangements to share the outcome of Phase Two and also to consider the next phase of the review process relating to deceased patients. The appropriate family engagement arrangements will be confirmed in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to Recommendation 52 of the Review of the implementation of the Welfare of Animals Act (NI) 2011, (i) for his assessment of the monitoring of staff to dog ratio conditions by local authorities in Wales; and (ii) if this will be introduced in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Existing legislation provides that dog breeding licences in Northern Ireland can only be granted by local councils following an inspection of relevant premises for compliance with licensing conditions. These conditions deal with matters such as the numbers of dogs at establishments as well as conditions relating to exercise, socialisation and enrichment.  Following the Review of the Implementation of the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, my Department strengthened its guidance to councils on the licencing of dog breeding establishments. The guidance now provides that breeding establishments must, at all times, ensure that adequate and competent staffing is provided at their establishments to ensure the satisfactory health, welfare and safety of all the dogs they keep. Given the safeguards in existing legislation and the bolstered guidance now in place, I am not convinced that there is need to introduce statutory provision prescribing staffing ratios in dog breeding establishments like those that exist in Wales. The other jurisdictions of the United Kingdom do not have legislation that specifies staff to dog ratios at such establishments and my Department is not aware of any immediate plans for legislation to regulate this matter there.    My Department is, however, intending to publish a plan detailing those animal welfare related legislation and policies to be progressed during this mandate. While the plan is still under development, it is intended that a review of the regulation of dog breeding establishments in Northern Ireland will form a key component of it.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when was the last time the three enforcement bodies met to discuss the enforcement of the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011; and (ii) what outcomes there have been from this.
answer_text: The Multi-Agency Animal Welfare Enforcement Forum, which comprises of the three animal welfare enforcement bodies in Northern Ireland, meets at least three times annually.  It last met on 16 June 2022. The Forum provides a useful platform for liaison between the enforcement bodies. It allows them to share best practice in enforcement and lessons learned from specific investigations.  As such, it helps ensure the consistency and efficiency of working practices in relation to animal welfare enforcement across all three bodies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to Recommendation 24 of the Review of the Implementation of the Welfare of Animals Act (2011) report, what protocols have the three enforcement bodies established regarding animal welfare issues.
answer_text: The Multi-Agency Animal Welfare Forum was established in 2016 following the Review of the Implementation of the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011. It comprises of representatives from the three animal welfare enforcement bodies in Northern Ireland, namely my Department, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) and local councils.  The Forum operates under Terms of Reference and has established a Memorandum of Understanding which, amongst other things, sets out the responsibilities of each enforcement body. My Department and the PSNI have separate information sharing agreements with councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when legislation will be amended to allow details contained within Council held registers of licensed dog breeding establishments to be made available to the public.
answer_text: I am not convinced that it is necessary to legislate to allow details contained within council-held dog breeding registers to be published. Consent to publish these details could be obtained by local councils from dog breeding establishments when they apply for, or seek renewal of, licences. There has already been engagement between my Department and some local councils on this potential approach, which is within the gift of councils themselves. My Officials will, however, raise it with council representatives for further discussion when the Local Government Animal Welfare Strategic Partnership Board next meets in October 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the reasons for the delay in a decision on the wind turbine at Knock Iveagh, which his Department previously advised would be made by 31 August 2022.
answer_text: I can confirm that on 5 September 2022 I wrote to the Chief Executive of Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Council to advise that the Department is of the view that the Council is best placed to take enforcement action relating to the wind turbine development at the Knock Iveagh site, if they choose to do so. The very slight delay of 3 working days was required to properly consider this decision.  It is now a matter for the Council on how to proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will announce the outcome of his Department’s determination in respect to the ongoing case relating to a wind turbine and ancillary development at Knock Iveagh, Rathriland.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering whether it is expedient for it to exercise its planning enforcement powers in relation to the wind turbine and ancillary development at Knock Iveagh and aims to make a decision on this no later than 31 August 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps her Department is taking to bring Knock Iveagh, Rathriland into state care.
answer_text: Knock Iveagh was statutorily protected as a scheduled historic monument in 1996, in recognition of its regional significance. Protection through scheduling means that the monument’s condition is monitored by officials from my Department and any proposed changes affecting the monument require the consent of my Department.  I have now asked officials to engage with relevant stakeholders and to consider all of the available options to ensure that this important site remains appropriately protected moving forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure on what date his Department last carried out dredging on the River Quoile in Downpatrick.
answer_text: My Department does not carry out routine dredging works on the River Quoile.  However, sporadic channel works on the River Quoile are carried out where there is an obstruction to the free flow of water. Works to clear partial blockages of the bridge upstream of Belfast Road, Downpatrick commenced on Monday 20 June 2022 and are currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to regulate private canine fertility clinics.
answer_text: Subject to some exceptions set out in law, only registered veterinary surgeons are permitted to medically treat animals. This applies to any medical treatment administered at canine fertility clinics.  It is also an offence for someone other than a registered veterinary surgeon to carry out any procedure that interferes with the sensitive tissues or bone structure of a dog. Again, this applies to any such procedures carried out at canine fertility clinics. Given these existing legal protections, my Department does not have any plans to bring forward additional legislation to regulate canine fertility clinics at this time. Any clinics that fall foul of the existing legal requirements are, however, liable to prosecution.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to ban circuses with wild animals.
answer_text: My officials are presently planning a schedule of animal welfare related legislation to be progressed during this mandate.  This will include legislation to prohibit the use of wild animals in circuses.  Any proposals on the matter will be subject to public consultation in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to ban the use of self-loosening snares.
answer_text: I have no immediate plans to reform the legislation or practises on the use of snares.  I understand that, although not widespread, there is still a need to retain self loosening snares as a practical method to control pest species in the countryside.  For example, some landowners use them to reduce the impact of foxes on new born lambs during the lambing season and gamekeepers also use them periodically to reduce the destruction of game birds by foxes at certain times of the year. Snares have also been utilised during conservation projects to protect vulnerable ground-nesting birds from predation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to require slaughterhouses to install CCTV in their premises.
answer_text: Over 99% of animals and birds processed in Northern Ireland are slaughtered under closed circuit television (CCTV) coverage. Departmental veterinarians also maintain a permanent presence in approved Northern Ireland slaughterhouses during the hours of slaughter and are responsible for monitoring animal welfare at the time of killing. 		Nevertheless, I can see merit in legislating to require all slaughterhouses in Northern Ireland to install CCTV at their premises in line with the approach that has, or is being taken, in other parts of the United Kingdom.		My officials are presently planning a schedule of animal welfare related legislation to be progressed during this mandate. This will include legislation requiring the use of CCTV. Any proposal for legislation on the matter will, of course, be subject to public consultation in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department currently holds a list of convicted animal abusers; and (ii) how many people are on this list, broken down by council area.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for instigating any prosecutions in respect of animal welfare offences relating to livestock.
