Local councils take prosecutions in respect of the welfare of companion animals while the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is responsible for prosecuting offences against wild animal or animal fighting offences. Details of any animal welfare convictions secured in Northern Ireland are recorded by the Department of Justice on its criminal records system. My Department does not hold a record or list of all persons convicted of animal welfare offences. It does, however, record details of any livestock animal welfare investigations it carries out, including any convictions secured. My Department uses the access it has to the Department of Justice’s system to ensure that persons disqualified from keeping or owning animals are not permitted to carry out any activities involving animals that are regulated by my Department. In addition, it publishes statistics relating to the outcomes of all welfare investigations including those taken by the PSNI and local councils on an annual basis.  The Report for 2021 will be available before the end of this year with all previous reports available here:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/statistics/animal-welfare-statistics.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to address illegal puppy breeding in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Responsibility for investigating and prosecuting alleged instances of illegal dog breeding in Northern Ireland rests with local councils. However, my Department has established and chairs the ‘Paws for Thought Group’ which co-ordinates the actions of key stakeholders to target the illegal breeding and trafficking of low welfare pups. My Department is also working closely with local councils and other relevant statutory bodies to effectively deliver an enhanced enforcement operation in respect of these activities at Belfast and Larne ports.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to rollout the microchipping and registration of kittens on the same basis that is currently done for puppies.
answer_text: My Department’s Code of Practice for the Welfare of Cats already recommends that owners should ensure their cats are identified using either collars or microchips. However, I am aware that the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) intends to take forward legislation to make the microchipping of cats mandatory in England. At this juncture, my Department has not identified any immediate need for the microchipping and registration of cats in Northern Ireland but will continue to keep the progression and Defra legislation under review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to introduce Lucy’s Law to regulate the third party sale of puppies in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am committed to introducing a version of Lucy’s Law, to ban the third party sale of pups, within the current mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the Department of Work and Pensions' proposed Fighting Fraud in the Welfare System legislation.
answer_text: There are currently no agreed proposals for legislation as this work is still in the policy development stages. Officials from the Department will continue to engage with DWP and all proposals will be reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department has taken to ensure (i) those in Northern Ireland, under the Homes for Ukraine scheme, can exchange their driving license for a NI driving license; and (ii) information on how to do so is published on the Driver and Vehicle Agency website.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of developing a new Designation Order to allow for the exchange of Ukrainian driving licences.  A draft of the amending legislation has been prepared and will be scrutinised by Departmental solicitors as soon as possible. The new Designation Order will be brought into operation at the earliest opportunity at which point the information on the NI Direct website will be updated accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Independent Neurology Inquiry, including a timescale for publication of the final report, and the reasons for the delay in the report’s publication.
answer_text: On 7th June 2022, the Independent Neurology Inquiry publicly announced that their Final Report will be published on the 21st June 2022. The Chair, Mr Brett Lockhart QC, will deliver a statement at 1100 on that date; this may be viewed via a live link on the home page of the Inquiry’s website www.neurologyinquiry.org.ukIn order to fulfil their Terms of Reference, the Inquiry Panel has a duty to carry out their work fairly and thoroughly, and to produce a reliable and evidence based report of their findings. This inevitably takes time; however, I understand that all necessary stages have now been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to address waiting list times for eye surgery, given the long term risks to sight of not urgently addressing issues such as retinal detachment and the lack of private surgery options.
answer_text: In June 2021, I published the Elective Care Framework setting out 55 actions to reduce waiting times.  The Framework highlights the need for additional capacity in order to reduce waiting lists. The Framework sets out a five year plan with firm, time bound proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future.The pandemic has had a negative impact on waiting times for many elective care services including for eye surgery. Although theatre access was severely impacted during the pandemic, efforts were made to retain appropriate access for time-critical eye surgeries, including retinal surgery. To this end, some lower risk cataract surgeries were displaced from Cataract Daycase Elective Care Centres to give access to higher acuity time-critical treatments, including trabeculectomies for glaucoma, with retinal detachment surgeries continuing to be delivered in the acute setting.Where in-house capacity was not available, arrangements were made to facilitate delivery of time-critical surgeries for HSC patients within the independent sector. This arrangement continues in 2022/23 with funding available for time-critical vitreoretinal procedures.Strenuous efforts are being made across the HSC to reduce the current long waiting times, including rebuilding activity in cataract day procedure centres and in the acute setting for more complex cases, and securing additional capacity by working with independent sector providers.  My Department has established robust monitoring arrangements to ensure that non-recurrent funding made available for elective waiting lists is utilised to see and treat as many patients as possible.   This non-recurrent funding of £46m for the first half of this year is additional to core funding for HSC baseline delivery. It is available for both in-house HSC-delivered care, and to fund additional capacity from the Independent Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to commission an acute service for people with eating disorders.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts work to the same regional ‘You in Mind Eating Disorder Care Pathway (2015)’ which uses a stepped model of care, commissioned to deal with the severe, enduring, and treatment resistant cases of Anorexia Nervosa, Bulimia Nervosa, and associated atypical eating disorders.  Services can range from targeted prevention, early intervention and supported self-help, specialist physical and mental health services, specialist eating disorder services, to highly intensive mental health services, which are usually intensive daily support and hospital based care.	Options for improving eating disorder service provision across Northern Ireland have been examined by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), formally the Health and Social Care Board, through the Regional Eating Disorders Network Group.  The findings of the review shaped the strategic policy direction reflected in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 that I published in June 2021. 		Action 29(d) of the Strategy commits to enhancing the regional eating disorder service and a business case to support this action is currently being developed by SPPG.  This will be a significant first step in improving mental health services and ensuring that those with eating disorders get the care and treatment they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to commission bariatric surgery for patients.
answer_text: At the beginning of 2019, my Department announced plans to explore options to develop a prototype Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS), including a Tier 4 bariatric surgery service, and a working group was set up to take this forward. However, as a result of the pandemic, the Department had to redirect significant resources to address all aspects of the immediate and critical issues associated with Covid-19, meaning that the work taken forward to develop the prototype, as with many other elements of work, had to be put on hold.  As we emerge from the pandemic, our immediate focus is on rebuilding the Health and Social Care (HSC) service. Currently, in the absence of a ROMS, the working group that was established to take this work forward has put in place arrangements to ensure assessment can be provided to those people presenting to HSC facilities with complications associated with obesity. In tandem with this, work is underway through the working group to ensure foundations are set in place to enable to the continuation of development work for a robust ROMS, for when resources are available. My Department is also currently working on the development of a new Obesity Strategy for Northern Ireland which is due to be published in 2023. The strategy will outline policy on both the prevention and treatment of obesity, including surgical intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps are being taken to ensure the Fire and Rescue Service establishes a formal record of controlled burning, to aid in the investigation of wildfires and related matters.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) formal record keeping of all calls received from landowners who inform NIFRS Regional Control Centre of their intent to complete a controlled burn will be effective from 1 September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps are being taken to introduce a licensing system for ‘at home’ dog boarding services, which fall outside of current legislation covering kennelling facilities.
answer_text: My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those who provide boarding from their homes, during this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
